examples.english: I admire your respect for old people.

etymology.text: From it the root ب خ خ (b ḵ ḵ).

body.text: , (S, A, K, &c.,) [in some copies of the K written
, which is wrong, for it is] like

, (TA,) [perhaps, as I have suggested above, from the sound made by a hecamel in a state of excitement,] a word used on the occasion of praising; (S, A;) on praising one from whom has proceeded a good and wonderful action; (Har p. 142;) on approving a thing; (T, S, Msb, K;) on being pleased with it, or having one's admiration excited by it; (A, K;) or on the occasion of glorying and of praising; (K;) in pronouncing a thing great in estimation, (IAmb,) or excellent; (AHeyth;) in deeming a thing great in estimation, (AHei,) or good; (Mgh;) or it means wonder, or admiration; (R;) and sometimes it is used [ironically] to denote disapproval; also, as an exhortation to gentleness with a thing, and to taking extraordinary pains; (TA;) and in a case of expertness, or skilfulness: (AHei:) it means
most excellent! how good! how goodly! well done! bravo!
great in estimation is the thing,

in his copy or copies of the K,] that this explanation is like an express assertion that it is a verb in the pret. tense, which requires consideration. (TA.) It is used alone; and in this case you say,
, (Msb, K,) with kesr for its invariable termination, (Msb,) and
; (K, TA; [but in the CK, in the place of
;]) without tesh-deed, (T, Msb,) in most cases; (Msb;) but also with teshdeed, (T, S, A,) like a noun; so that one says,
]: (S:). and one repeats it, (S, A, K, &c.,) for the sake of emphasis; (S, A;) saying,
, (S, A, R, K,) pronounced in the latter manner, with tenween, when in connexion with a following word, [and in this case only, whereas it is pronounced in the former manner in any case,] (S, A,) and
